questions.question: Which statement best defines a disaster from a health-system perspective?
options: A. Any event involving more than 100 casualties
B. An event in which needs exceed the immediately available resources
C. Any weather-related emergency
D. An event declared by the federal government
questions.rationale: The health-system definition centers on the imbalance between needs and resources. Casualty counts, weather, or declarations do not define it — a small event can be a disaster where resources are scarce.

questions.question: What is the correct order of the four phases of the disaster cycle?
options: A. Response, Recovery, Mitigation, Preparedness
B. Mitigation, Preparedness, Response, Recovery
C. Preparedness, Mitigation, Recovery, Response
D. Detection, Response, Recovery, Review
questions.rationale: The classic cycle runs Mitigation → Preparedness → Response → Recovery, then feeds back into mitigation for the next event.

questions.question: Installing flood barriers and enforcing seismic building codes are examples of which phase?
options: A. Response
B. Recovery
C. Mitigation
D. Triage
questions.rationale: Mitigation comprises actions taken to reduce the impact of future events — structural and policy measures like barriers and building codes.

questions.question: The PRE-DISASTER Paradigm emphasizes which pre-event elements?
options: A. Planning and practice, resilience, and education and training
B. Triage, treatment, and transport
C. Detection and evacuation
D. Response and recovery only
questions.rationale: PRE-DISASTER frames readiness before an event: exercised plans, resilient people and infrastructure, and competency-based education such as the NDLS courses.

questions.question: How do care priorities change in a disaster compared with daily emergency care?
options: A. They do not change
B. From maximal care for each individual to the greatest good for the greatest number
C. From triage to first-come, first-served
D. All patients receive identical care
questions.rationale: Disaster care allocates limited resources to benefit the most patients. Triage, deferred care, and abbreviated documentation follow from this shift.

questions.question: Why does the same tornado count as a disaster for a rural hospital but not for a metropolitan trauma system?
options: A. Because tornado severity varies by region
B. Because the definition depends on the balance between needs and locally available resources
C. Because rural hospitals use different triage colors
D. Because federal declarations differ
questions.rationale: Disaster is relative to resources: casualties that overwhelm a small facility may be absorbed routinely by a large system.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the all-hazards approach?
options: A. To write a unique plan for every conceivable event
B. To use one common management framework across all disaster types, with hazard-specific annexes
C. To focus planning only on the most likely hazard
D. To eliminate the need for training
questions.rationale: All-hazards planning builds one flexible framework that applies to natural, explosive, chemical, biological, and radiological events, supplemented by specific annexes — more robust and efficient than separate plans.

questions.question: A hospital's disaster plan is thorough but has never been exercised. What is the main risk?
options: A. None — a written plan is sufficient
B. The plan will fail under stress because gaps were never revealed and staff lack practiced roles
C. The plan will expire legally
D. Exercises are only for EMS agencies
questions.rationale: Exercising is what converts paper plans into performance: it exposes gaps, builds familiarity, and creates the muscle memory needed during a real response.

questions.question: Which is a component of a responder's personal preparedness?
options: A. A family communication plan and go-kit
B. Memorizing every federal regulation
C. Owning specialized rescue vehicles
D. Avoiding disaster training to stay flexible
questions.rationale: Personal preparedness — communication plan, go-kit, dependent care arrangements, knowledge of workplace roles — keeps responders able to report for duty.

questions.question: Why is staff personal preparedness a facility-level concern?
options: A. It is not — it is purely private
B. Surge capacity depends on staff reporting for duty, and staff with unprepared families often cannot
C. It reduces insurance premiums
D. It replaces the need for a hospital plan
questions.rationale: A hospital's surge plan assumes staff availability. Personal and family readiness directly affects whether the workforce shows up in a crisis.

questions.question: A mass-casualty incident (MCI) is best defined as:
options: A. Any incident with fatalities
B. An event producing more patients than local resources can manage using routine procedures
C. An incident involving hazardous materials
D. Any incident requiring more than one ambulance
questions.rationale: The MCI definition parallels the disaster definition: patient load beyond what routine local procedures can handle.

questions.question: During a disaster response, a provider is asked to work in a role outside their usual daily assignment, under incident command. This is:
options: A. A violation of disaster principles
B. An expected feature of disaster response within the command structure
C. Only allowed for physicians
D. Evidence of a failed plan
questions.rationale: Disasters routinely require flexible role assignment under incident command; preparing providers for this shift is part of BDLS training.

questions.question: Which activity belongs to the RECOVERY phase?
options: A. Field triage of casualties
B. Restoring health-system function and community services after the event, potentially over years
C. Stockpiling supplies before an event
D. Building levees
questions.rationale: Recovery restores the community and health system to normal or improved function. Triage is response; stockpiling is preparedness; levees are mitigation.

questions.question: Immunization campaigns that reduce a community's vulnerability to epidemics are an example of:
options: A. Response
B. Mitigation
C. Triage
D. Evacuation
questions.rationale: Reducing the future impact of a hazard — including biological hazards — is mitigation.

questions.question: Health professionals converging on a disaster scene without being requested or integrated into incident command are:
options: A. Always helpful
B. A known hazard — self-dispatched responders complicate scene management and safety
C. Required by law
D. The preferred staffing method
questions.rationale: Spontaneous, unintegrated responders burden scene safety, accountability, and coordination. Responding through the system — when requested, into an assigned role — is the professional standard.

questions.question: Which best captures why mitigation and preparedness 'save more lives than response'?
options: A. Response is unimportant
B. Pre-event actions shrink the needs-versus-resources gap before it opens, preventing casualties that no response could later recover
C. Responders are usually unavailable
D. It is a slogan without basis
questions.rationale: Codes that keep buildings standing, plans that speed evacuation, and trained staff prevent deaths outright; even a perfect response can only salvage what the event has already done.
